steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence (Quantitative/Textual) question. Morales and colleagues concluded that snowball sampling "may be superior" to purposive sampling for recruiting rural participants. A supporting finding must directly show snowball sampling outperforming purposive sampling on the relevant measure: recruiting participants with desired characteristics.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Snowball sampling yielded significantly more participants with the desired characteristics than purposive sampling did.: This is a direct head-to-head comparison showing snowball sampling produced more qualified participants than purposive sampling — exactly the evidence needed to support the conclusion that snowball sampling is superior. ✓
  • Choice B — Snowball sampling yielded significantly more participants than would be expected if researchers contacted people without regard to those people's characteristics.: This compares snowball sampling to a random baseline, not to purposive sampling. It says nothing about how the two techniques compare to each other. ✗
  • Choice C — Participants recruited through snowball sampling were more likely to have taken part in previous studies than were participants recruited through purposive sampling.: Prior study participation is unrelated to whether a recruitment technique is superior for finding participants with desired characteristics. ✗
  • Choice D — Most participants recruited through snowball sampling had different characteristics than the initial participants who recruited them.: This actually suggests a weakness of snowball sampling — the sample drifts from the desired characteristics — and would undermine, not support, the conclusion. ✗
That matches Choice A — Snowball sampling yielded significantly more participants with the desired characteristics than purposive sampling did..
